banner: Week 20 · The Pythagorean Theorem — Legs, Hypotenuse & a² + b² = c²
essential_question: If you know a right triangle's two legs, is there an exact way — no measuring tape needed — to find its third side?
objective: Identify the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle, state the Pythagorean theorem (a² + b² = c²), use it to find a missing side while keeping radical answers in simplest form, and use its converse to test whether three given lengths form a right triangle.

content.prompt: A ramp needs to rise 3 ft over a 4 ft horizontal run. Without ever measuring the ramp itself, is there a way to find its exact length using only those two numbers? What would you even do with them?

agenda.title: I do — Legs, Hypotenuse & the Theorem

content.heading: One equation, every right triangle
bullets: A right triangle has exactly one 90° angle — mark it with a small square.
The two sides that form the right angle are the legs (a and b); the side opposite the right angle, always the longest, is the hypotenuse (c).
The Pythagorean theorem: a² + b² = c² — square each leg, add them, and you get the hypotenuse squared.
To find a missing side, solve for the unknown and take the square root — keep the radical in simplest form unless it's a perfect square.
The converse: if a² + b² = c² is true for three given sides (c the longest), the triangle IS a right triangle; if not, it isn't.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag the Legs, Watch the Squares

content.heading: Move a leg; watch the squares balance
content.note: On the sim, drag the handle at the end of leg a or leg b. Watch the square drawn on each side grow, and watch their areas update live — does the sum of the two smaller squares always equal the big tilted one? Try to land on 3 and 4. Then try two legs that don't give a whole-number hypotenuse — what does the sim do with the radical?

agenda.title: You do — Find the Missing Side

content.preview: Build session: build a 3-4-5 triangle with string and confirm the right angle really is 90°.

success_criteria.text: I can identify the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle and state the Pythagorean theorem
I can use a² + b² = c² to find a missing side, keeping radical answers in simplest form
I can use the converse of the Pythagorean theorem to test whether three lengths form a right triangle

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W20 The Pythagorean Theorem.md

questions.prompt: The two sides that form the right angle in a right triangle are called the…
questions.explain: The legs are the two sides that meet at the 90° angle; the hypotenuse is the side opposite it.

questions.prompt: The side opposite the right angle — always the longest side in a right triangle — is called the…
questions.explain: The hypotenuse sits opposite the 90° angle and is always the longest of the three sides.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs measuring 5 and 12. Find the length of the hypotenuse.
On the grid, draw a right triangle with legs 3 units and 4 units, mark the right angle, and use the Pythagorean theorem to find and label the hypotenuse.
questions.answer: A right triangle with legs 3 and 4 labeled, the right angle marked, and the hypotenuse labeled 5 (since 3² + 4² = 25 and √25 = 5).
questions.explain: Rubric: a right triangle with legs 3 and 4 and the right angle marked (1 pt) + the hypotenuse correctly computed and labeled 5 (1 pt).

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs measuring 4 and 6. Find the exact length of the hypotenuse, simplifying the radical.
Could a triangle with sides 9, 12, and 16 be a right triangle? Use the converse of the Pythagorean theorem to justify your answer.
questions.answer: No — 9² + 12² = 81 + 144 = 225, but 16² = 256. Since 225 ≠ 256, the triangle is not right.
questions.explain: The converse checks whether the two shorter sides squared and added equal the longest side squared; here they don't, so the triangle can't be right.

questions.prompt: Exit: name one place you'd use the Pythagorean theorem in real life (a ladder against a wall, a TV screen's diagonal, squaring a foundation's corner) and say which of your three measurements would be the hypotenuse.
